L1.area: Mathematical data types and arithmetic operations

L2.description: A misalignment in the maximum allowed bit-length between integer and decimal data types causes an inconsistency in range validation. Decimal values can be created that fit within the decimal type's validation bounds but exceed the valid range of the corresponding integer type. When such an oversized decimal value is used in an integer context, it causes a panic. An attacker can exploit this by providing crafted numeric inputs that produce values within the incorrectly permissive decimal range but outside the actual safe limits, leading to a denial of service through an unrecoverable panic.

L3.area: Mathematical data types and arithmetic operations
L3.description: A misalignment in the maximum allowed bit-length between integer and decimal data types causes an inconsistency in range validation. Decimal values can be created that fit within the decimal type's validation bounds but exceed the valid range of the corresponding integer type. When such an oversized decimal value is used in an integer context, it causes a panic. An attacker can exploit this by providing crafted numeric inputs that produce values within the incorrectly permissive decimal range but outside the actual safe limits, leading to a denial of service through an unrecoverable panic.

ground_truth.reason: The maxDecBitLen constant used for range validation in decimal arithmetic operations was misaligned with MaxBitLen used for integers, allowing decimal values that exceed the integer type's valid range and causing panics during type interactions.
